Model Context Protocol explained: the open standard that gives LLMs access to YOUR data

4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

AI is an ever-growing field in the tech industry, with more and more tools being adapted and updated for use by AI systems. A key issue presents itself though with such an expansive variety of tools: integration.
Modern LLMs still face two key limitations:

• Their knowledge is limited to the data they were trained on.

• They have difficulty interacting with multiple external systems.
The Model Context Protocol (MCP), introduced by Anthropic in November 2024, was made to address this issue by providing a consistent connection framework for AI systems to connect with external applications and databases.
Quick Answer: Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that enables LLMs to integrate with multiple external applications and databases through a common, standardized protocol.
What is Model Context Protocol (MCP)?
Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard that creates a two-way communication pathway between AI systems and external services, allowing AI models to retrieve real-time information and perform actions beyond what they were originally trained for.
By standardizing API integrations, MCP removes the need to build custom integrations for every external system.
In Simple Terms
Without MCP, AI applications often lack direct access to an organization’s databases, APIs, and internal systems. As a result, outputs can become generic or require significant edits.
With MCP, AI applications can access these databases and workflows to produce more accurate, immediate, and relevant results.
Quote: “MCP standardizes how AI systems connect to external tools, databases, and workflows.”
Why Does MCP Matter?
MCP matters because it bridges the gap between AI applications and access to the context they need for complex projects that require the use of external tools and databases of proprietary / confidential information.
AI platforms like Cursor, Windsurf, and Cline can generate code, automate tasks, and answer questions, but they often lack direct access to business systems.
Quick Summary
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image By AuthorWhat Are the Benefits of MCP?
MCP-integrations for your AI application allows you to benefit from more reliability in outputs, a shared integration standard for your business’ tools and databases, and the convenience of pre-built connections with the majority of popular tools and data-storage solutions.
Greater Reliability in Outputs
MCP allows LLMs to connect to proprietary data sources, reducing hallucinations and improving accuracy.
This makes AI responses more reliable for:

• Strategic business decisions

• Complex research

• Detailed planning

• Goal setting using available business resources and data
Greater Integration with Business Tools and Workflows
MCP enables AI systems to integrate with APIs, CRMs, databases, and other business tools.
This allows AI to:

• Update records

• Automate basic tasks

• Handle complete processes instead of simply generating text
Pre-built Integrations
MCP simplifies integration by providing a single common communication layer.
Businesses can build an AI technology stack that is easier to scale by adding or removing tools as needed.
MCP also provides pre-built integrations for systems including:

• Google Drive

• Slack

• GitHub

• Postgres
It also supports flexibility across different AI providers.
Quote: “MCP creates a scalable foundation for enterprise AI by creating a shared integration standard.”
How Do You Implement MCP?
Well, let's break this down into a series of steps;

• Identify the data sources and tools your application needs to complete the assigned process.

• Expose these tools, workflows, applications, or databases through an MCP server. The server defines what actions are available, including:
-Querying a data base
- Reading documents
- Triggering workflows And more. These opportunities are identified using the MCP Standard Interface.

• Configure your AI agent (the MCP Client) and connect it to one or more MCP servers. The client follows the MCP specifications, discovers its available capabilities, and interacts with them as needed.

• Combine the LLM with the connected tools into an agent workflow. The result is an AI system with successful integrations and greater reliability. The final step is choosing the right MCP server for your requirements.
And with that you have an AI System with a series of successful integrations, and ultimately, more reliability. All you really need to do to polish your system any further is to pick the right MCP server depending on your needs. Choosing the right MCP architecture depends on your security requirements, infrastructure, and business workflows. Genetech Solutions helps businesses by hosting and deploying secure, scalable servers for MCP-powered AI systems tailored to their operational needs.
Key Takeaways

• MCP standardizes AI integration with external applications and databases.

• It enables AI systems to retrieve real-time information and perform actions beyond their training.

• It improves reliability by connecting LLMs to proprietary data sources.

• It simplifies integration through a common protocol and pre-built integrations.

• It allows organizations to build scalable AI workflows with connected tools and services.
Conclusion
MCP standardizes how AI models integrate with external data sources, applications, and services. It enables LLMs in corporate AI systems to produce more accurate responses, handle more complex automation, and save time and resources through simple, scalable integrations.
Organizations that adopt MCP for their AI workflows will be better positioned to build scalable, secure, and intelligent AI solutions.
